reimagine.name: Reimagine (AWS Transform + Claude Code)
reimagine.description: Reverse-engineer the mainframe to extract structured business rules and domain decomposition, then forward-engineer entirely new cloud-native microservices from those extractions. Distinct from Refactor (which preserves the monolithic architecture via deterministic COBOL-to-Java conversion) — Reimagine reshapes the architecture itself.
reimagine.best_for: Applications where the target architecture must be event-driven / API-first / real-time (not just cloud-hosted); where original engineers are retiring and rebuilding institutional knowledge as extracted business rules is a first-class outcome; where the business is willing to accept process change to unlock digital channels
reimagine.timeline: 18-36 months per application (reverse-engineer phase 3-6 months, forward-engineer + validate 12-30 months)
reimagine.cost_savings: Variable — long-term architectural agility rather than pure infra cost reduction. Post-migration cloud infra typically 60-80% below mainframe TCO; larger benefits accrue from faster feature delivery and channel expansion.
fsi_use_cases: Digital banking platforms requiring event-driven processing across every channel (not nightly batch)
Core banking replacement where terminal screens must become cloud-native APIs
Real-time claims / underwriting pipelines replacing batch settlement
Government benefits / entitlement systems moving from days-to-hours SLA
risks: Requires Human-in-the-Loop validation at every phase — AI-generated specifications MUST be reviewed by application SMEs before code generation
Highest upfront investment in reverse-engineering artifacts
Domain decomposition errors compound: bad bounded-context boundaries propagate through microservice design
steps: Phase 1 — Reverse Engineer (AWS Transform for mainframe): agents analyze COBOL, PL/I, JCL, copybooks, and data stores; produce structured business documentation, extract business rules with Given/When/Then acceptance criteria, decompose monolith into bounded contexts by business domain, and map data lineage + data dictionary
Phase 1 output: artifacts land in s3://<bucket>/transform-output/<job-id>/ with subdirs business-documentation/, data_analysis/, decomposition/, documentation/, inputs/, smf-analysis/

Phase 2 — Forward Engineer (Claude Code + agentic skills): download AWS Transform artifacts to local workspace; configure CLAUDE.md with @imports for project context and methodology; invoke skills like /bre-analysis, /ddd-analysis, /spec-generation, /database-generation, /microservice-code-generation, /iac-generation
Phase 2 validation gate: application SMEs REVIEW and validate the AI-generated specifications BEFORE any code generation. This human-in-the-loop step is non-negotiable per AWS guidance.

Phase 2 code generation: after spec sign-off, generate the microservices code + IaC (CDK), targeting any of Java (Spring Boot), Python, .NET, etc. — technology-stack choice belongs to the customer, not the tool
Phase 3 — Test & Deploy: automated testing (unit / integration) plus human verification via code review, integration testing, and user acceptance testing
Phase 3 cutover: incremental domain-by-domain cutover from mainframe monolith to microservices with parallel-run windows sized to the risk tier of each domain

rehost.name: Rehost (Lift and Shift)
rehost.description: Move mainframe workloads to AWS with minimal code changes using emulation. NEW-CUSTOMER STATUS NOTE: AWS Mainframe Modernization Managed Runtime (Micro Focus) closed to new customers 2025-11-07. Rehost paths for new customers today go through Rocket Software (which acquired the Micro Focus mainframe business in mid-2024) — either self-managed on EC2/containers, or via the AWS-Rocket partnership.
aws_services: AWS Mainframe Modernization Managed Runtime (existing customers only)
partner_tools: Rocket Software Enterprise Server (former Micro Focus)
rehost.best_for: Quick migration, low risk tolerance, limited modernization budget
risks: New customers must use Rocket Software or partner tooling — M2 Managed Runtime not available
steps: Assess current mainframe workloads and dependencies
Choose runtime: self-managed on EC2/containers, or Rocket Software / partner-managed
Configure runtime (Rocket Enterprise Server or partner equivalent) on AWS
Migrate COBOL/JCL/CICS artifacts
Validate functional equivalence
Performance test and tune
Cutover with rollback plan

replatform.name: Replatform (Augmented Migration)
replatform.description: Move to AWS with targeted optimizations - convert data stores, modernize interfaces
steps: Inventory and classify workloads
Design target AWS architecture
Convert data stores (DB2 to Aurora, VSAM to DynamoDB)
Modernize interfaces (CICS screens to APIs)
Implement CI/CD pipeline
Parallel run and validate
Phased cutover

refactor.name: Refactor (Re-architect)
refactor.description: Rewrite mainframe applications as cloud-native microservices
refactor.best_for: Maximum cloud benefits, long-term cost optimization, agility
steps: Decompose monolith into bounded contexts
Design microservices architecture
Rewrite business logic in modern language (Java/Python)
Implement event-driven patterns
Build automated testing suite
Deploy with blue/green strategy
Decommission mainframe components incrementally

retire.name: Retire (Decommission)
retire.description: Identify and shut down mainframe workloads no longer needed
steps: Identify candidate workloads with low/no usage
Verify no upstream/downstream dependencies
Archive data to S3 Glacier per retention policies
Document decommission for audit trail
Remove from mainframe

automated_refactor.name: Automated Refactor (AWS Transform for mainframe)
automated_refactor.description: AI-assisted deterministic transformation of COBOL/PL/I/JCL/CICS/Db2/VSAM to Java (Spring Boot) + PostgreSQL / Groovy. Preserves the application's monolithic shape while modernizing the language and runtime. Distinct from Reimagine (which reshapes architecture into microservices) and Refactor-by-hand. AWS Transform for mainframe reached GA on 2025-05-15.
partner_tools: AWS Transform (built on Blu Age technology, formerly Q Developer Transform)
automated_refactor.best_for: Large COBOL/PL/I codebases needing systematic language conversion while preserving business rules
automated_refactor.cost_savings: 50-70% infrastructure cost reduction (customer-reported: Itaú ~90% time reduction on discovery; Visma ~55% annual cost savings)
risks: Generated code quality requires Human-in-the-Loop validation
Manual tuning still required for niche patterns
Monolithic shape preserved — not the same as Reimagine
steps: Phase 1 — Discovery: AWS Transform agents analyze COBOL/PL/I codebase (cyclomatic complexity, dependency graph)
Phase 2 — Planning: define target architecture, review Business Logic Extraction (BLE) outputs
Phase 3 — Code Conversion: automated COBOL → Java (Spring Boot), JCL → Groovy, Db2 → PostgreSQL
Phase 4 — Human-in-the-Loop validation of generated specifications and code (non-negotiable per AWS guidance)
Phase 5 — Testing: Functional Equivalence Testing against original mainframe behavior
Phase 6 — Deployment: production cutover with parallel-run windows
Optional: Reforge step to enhance the refactored code for maintainability
automated_refactor.historical_note: This pattern was previously delivered via 'AWS Mainframe Modernization (Blu Age)' + 'CodeWhisperer' + 'Q Developer Transform' — those names are all superseded. Blu Age technology is now under AWS Transform; CodeWhisperer merged into Amazon Q Developer late 2024; Q Developer Transform was rebranded AWS Transform at GA 2025-05-15.
